name: Roombots
short_description: Self-reconfiguring modular robotic blocks that connect and move to assemble themselves into reconfigurable, adaptive furniture.
long_description: Roombots are homogeneous modular robots developed at EPFL's Biorobotics Laboratory as building blocks for furniture that can move, change shape, and reconfigure itself during the day. Each module has rotational degrees of freedom for locomotion and active connection mechanisms that let many modules attach and detach at runtime to form chairs, tables, and other structures. As a vision of physical AI, Roombots reframes the built environment itself as an actuated, reconfigurable medium, where the furniture of a room becomes a distributed swarm of robots that rearrange around human needs rather than static objects.
ai_note: Locomotion uses central pattern generator controllers whose parameters are tuned by online machine-learning optimization (stochastic approximation / SPSA) so modules learn efficient gaits for changing structures; this is learned/optimized control, not deep learning.

images.caption: Roombots modular robotic modules connected to form a self-reconfiguring furniture structure at EPFL BioRob.
images.alt_text: Several cube-and-cylinder shaped robotic modules linked together into a furniture-like structure in a robotics lab.

sources.title: Roombots: reconfigurable robots for adaptive furniture
Learning to Move in Modular Robots using Central Pattern Generators and Online Optimization
sources.publisher: The International Journal of Robotics Research

sources.title: Roombot Swarm Creates On-Demand Mobile Furniture

interactions.title: Modules crawl across a connector grid to assemble furniture
interactions.description: Several Roombot modules rotate end-over-end and grip into a grid of connector plates on the floor and walls, climbing into position and latching to each other and to passive panels to build up a stool, chair, or table.

interactions.title: A piece of furniture reconfigures itself over the day
interactions.description: The same set of modules changes role as needs change — a stool grows into a chair, or a row of chairs merges into a sofa — by detaching and reconnecting rather than being replaced.

interactions.title: Modules learn an efficient gait for a new shape
interactions.description: When modules form a new structure like a tripod or snake, central pattern generator controllers are tuned online by stochastic optimization so the assembly discovers an efficient way to locomote across the floor.

interactions.title: Two modules cooperate to climb over a corner
interactions.description: One module braces and lifts while a second swings over the edge of a step or corner, letting the pair traverse 3D obstacles that a single module could not climb alone.
interactions.source_label: Robotics and Autonomous Systems — hardware paper

interactions.title: An ordinary IKEA table gets up and walks to you
interactions.description: Roombot modules clipped onto the legs of a normal table turn it into mobile furniture that walks to a chosen spot, demonstrated alongside a jamming gripper that lets a module pick small objects up off the floor.

interactions.title: Furniture summoned, dismissed, or shooed by gesture
interactions.description: With overhead Kinect hand tracking, a person waves a stool over to sit on or sends it away — including the demo of 'chairs that flee from you' — closing the loop between human gesture and furniture that comes and goes.

interactions.title: An older adult asks the room to help them stand
interactions.description: Grounded in the lab's assistive-furniture vision, a resident recovering from a fall could have nearby modules grow into a graded sequence of supports — low cushion to seat to standing rail — guiding the transition between lying, sitting, and standing.

interactions.title: A conference room that re-lays itself out between meetings
interactions.description: Between bookings the modular benches and tables crawl across the connector grid to switch the room from rows to a circle to clustered pods, matching the next session's format without anyone moving furniture by hand.
